result:
The first frost of the season always arrives without fanfare. One morning the air simply tastes different—sharper, cleaner, like someone has peeled the summer off the world and left the raw nerves exposed. I step onto the back porch with coffee in hand and watch my breath bloom into small ghosts that dissolve before they can tell any secrets.

There’s something quietly magnificent about the way November strips things down. The trees surrender their colors with surprising grace, as if they’ve been waiting all year for this honesty. No more pretending to be eternal. Just bare branches sketching charcoal lines against a pearl-colored sky. The garden has collapsed into brown lace, yet a few stubborn marigolds still burn defiant orange against the decay. I admire their refusal to accept the obvious.

Inside, the house is starting to smell like winter: cinnamon, woodsmoke, old books, and the peculiar scent of radiators waking up after months of sleep. I’ve begun the annual ritual of rearranging my bookshelves—not because they need it, but because touching old favorites feels like checking in on distant friends. Vonnegut smiles at me with that same tired optimism. Atwood watches with her usual clear-eyed warning. They’ve all been here before. They know how this story goes.

Winter isn’t coming. It’s already here, moving softly through the rooms while we pretend not to notice. There’s a strange comfort in that. In a world that never stops shouting, the quiet season arrives like an old friend who doesn’t need you to perform. Just sit here, it says. Breathe. Remember what matters when nothing is blooming.

(Word count: 248)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
